questions.title: Basic Auth
questions.question:
Basic Auth

I see that JWT authentications and sessions are documented, how would a basic auth be implemented?

I have an Authentication object with the username and a list of permissions.

The idea I had was to connect to an ldap, validate those credentials, get the user permissions and then depending on the method validate one or the other. I have everything implemented except the rest api part. 

I had thought about implementing a custom authentication, but I don't see it documented in the openapi for example.
I honestly don't know where to start.
questions.reference_answer:
Litestar doesn't ship a Basic Auth scheme out of the box, but the building blocks are there. Subclass `AbstractAuthenticationMiddleware` and add a `SecurityScheme` entry in the OpenAPI config for the doc side.

Sketch:

```python
import base64
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.connection import ASGIConnection
from litestar.exceptions import NotAuthorizedException
from litestar.middleware import AbstractAuthenticationMiddleware, AuthenticationResult
from litestar.openapi.config import OpenAPIConfig
from litestar.openapi.spec import Components, SecurityScheme


class BasicAuthMiddleware(AbstractAuthenticationMiddleware):
    async def authenticate_request(self, connection: ASGIConnection) -> AuthenticationResult:
        header = connection.headers.get("Authorization", "")
        if not header.startswith("Basic "):
            raise NotAuthorizedException("missing Basic auth")
        try:
            raw = base64.b64decode(header.removeprefix("Basic ")).decode("utf-8")
            username, _, password = raw.partition(":")
        except Exception as exc:
            raise NotAuthorizedException("invalid Basic auth") from exc

        user = await your_ldap_validate(username, password)
        if user is None:
            raise NotAuthorizedException("invalid credentials")

        return AuthenticationResult(user=user, auth=None)
```

Wire it up:

```python
app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[your_handlers],
    middleware=[BasicAuthMiddleware],
    openapi_config=OpenAPIConfig(
        title="API",
        version="1.0.0",
        components=Components(
            security_schemes={"basic_auth": SecurityScheme(type="http", scheme="basic")}
        ),
        security=[{"basic_auth": []}],
    ),
)
```

From running this kind of setup in prod, two things bit me.

`BasicAuthMiddleware` needs an exclude list for unauthenticated routes like `/health` and `/docs`. The middleware base accepts an exclude pattern that you'll want to configure.

LDAP roundtrips per request kill latency. Cache the validated user object (username plus a hash of the supplied password plus the permissions list) with a short TTL of 30 to 60 seconds.

The session and JWT middleware sources in `litestar.security` are the cleanest reference for the shape your subclass should take.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question with substantive answer showing how to implement Basic Auth by subclassing AbstractAuthenticationMiddleware and configuring OpenAPI, directly supported by chunks covering the middleware base class, OpenAPI configuration pattern, wiring, and AuthenticationResult return type.

questions.title: Why does the framework raise a 500 instead of a 400
questions.reference_answer:
Because the `ValueError` is just a regular exception. Litestar doesn't know about it. How could Litestar infer that this `ValueError` has to do with input data validation and should map to a `400` response?

You can either raise `ValidationError`, raise a `ClientError` directly, or create your own exception class and register an error handler for it.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete, self-contained litestar error-handling question with a substantive answer; chunks [0], [1], [2] address exception handling infrastructure and show how raising appropriate exception types maps to HTTP status codes.

questions.title: Could this line in litestar be simplified? Are the type hints correct?
questions.question:
Could this line in litestar be simplified? Are the type hints correct?

I have [this exact line from PrometheusMiddleware](https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/main/litestar/plugins/prometheus/middleware.py#L122C13-L122C108) in my own middleware,
```python
request = Request[Any, Any, Any](scope, receive)
method: str = request.method if request.scope["type"] == ScopeType.HTTP else request.scope["type"]
```
and when I enabled the mypy error code ["redundant-expr"](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/error_code_list2.html#check-that-expression-is-redundant-redundant-expr), that line was flagged, because request.scope["type"] is _always_ `ScopeType.HTTP`, see https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/main/litestar/connection/request.py#L65 and https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/main/litestar/types/asgi_types.py#L149

So if the type hints are correct, I should just be able to change this to
```python
request = Request[Any, Any, Any](scope, receive)
method: str = request.method
```
and it would be equivalent.

So the question is, is this correct? So that line in the litestar PrometheusMiddleware could also be simplified? I would just change it, but given that litestar has that line in their code, I am worried I will break something somewhere.
questions.reference_answer:
I believe the more correct way to express this would be 

```python
if connection.scope["type"] == ScopeType.HTTP:
 request = Request[Any, Any, Any](scope)
 method = request.method
else:
 method = scope["type"]
```
since it doesn't make sense to create a request for a non-http connection.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] demonstrates the exact middleware pattern recommended in the accepted answer (checking scope type before creating Request), and chunk [2] explains the scope structure that makes this pattern necessary.

questions.title: Making the OpenAPI docs only available to authenticated users
questions.question:
Making the OpenAPI docs only available to authenticated users

I am wondering if there is a known approach to making the docs private. I tried creating a subclass of OpenAPIController with guards specified, but this approach did not seem to work for me. The guard I am using works for other controllers, but when applied to the OpenAPIController, there is an issue with the guard trying to access "auth" in scope that is not there for some reason.

 I am open to other approaches, but for the sake of discussion, here is what I have:

The error when trying to access any of the schema endpoints:

```
raise ImproperlyConfiguredException("'auth' is not defined in scope, install an AuthMiddleware to set it")
litestar.exceptions.http_exceptions.ImproperlyConfiguredException: 500: 'auth' is not defined in scope, install an AuthMiddleware to set it
```

What I did to get here:

```
from litestar.openapi import OpenAPIController

class CustomDocController(OpenAPIController):
    guards = [requires(ClientAuthorizations.API)]
    middleware = [session_auth.middleware] # I've tried it with and without this specified. Same error

openapi_controller = CustomDocController
```
That controller is specified in the OpenAPI config, and then:

```
app = Litestar(
    ...
    on_app_init=[session_auth.on_app_init],
    openapi_config=openapi_config,
)
```
It may be worth noting that `session_auth` is a SessionAuth that uses a custom middleware subclassed from SessionAuthMiddleware, but it is working otherwise.

Also, here is the guard:

```
def requires(auth):
    def client_guard(
        connection: ASGIConnection, _: BaseRouteHandler
    ) -> None:
        if not auth in connection.auth: # <-- this is raising: 'auth' is not defined in scope, although this works for other controllers
            raise NotAuthorizedException
    return client_guard
```

Again, maybe there is just a standard way to do this and I can chuck all of the above. Otherwise, any thoughts on going about troubleshooting this would be much appreciated.

Thanks!
questions.reference_answer:
It turns out that I had "/schema" as an exclude path on my SessionAuth instance. I removed that and my approach above now seems to be working. The gist of it is simply to subclass OpenAPIController and provide it with whatever guards you would normally use on a controller for your authentication approach:

```
from litestar.openapi import OpenAPIController

class CustomDocController(OpenAPIController):
    guards = [myguard]
```

Then specify that as the `openapi_controller` on the OpenAPIConfig.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete configuration question about protecting OpenAPI docs with authentication; chunk [10] directly addresses the excluding/including endpoints mechanism that caused the problem (the '/schema' exclude path).

questions.title: Implementing a Payload Validation after the Response Body is generated with a token
questions.question:
Implementing a Payload Validation after the Response Body is generated with a token

I'm relatively new to using Litestar so forgive me if I am missing something. I am working on a project where I need to validate the response data against user roles after the response has been made. 

Essentially the flow is Request with Access token -> Grab data from a database -> Build Response body -> Validate the roles from the Access Token match the required roles from the data -> Return Response/Raise 403. 

I know that this isn't normally how one would approach validating a user's ability to access data, but due to the legacy systems that is being used, this was the approach my team and I determined was best for our use case.

Based on what I understood about Litestar, I had initially tried to use the after_request hook to grab the response before it was returned, and validate it there. This led to any issue because the ASGI connection was not passed with the response body in the after_request. 

I then tried to use a contextvar that would put the token into a custom class. I used a guard on my controller that would set the contextvar and tried to get it in the after_request function. I could not figure out why, but the contextvar would save with the guard, but it would not be returned when I called it from the after_request. 

The approach I am looking at now is making a custom Middleware protocol that will grab the token from the app and perform the role verification there before sending the response. I think this approach will work, but I am not sure if this is the proper way to implement the validation I want to do. 

If anyone has any insights into what I could have been doing wrong in my various approaches or if there is a better one, I would greatly appreciate some advice. I am still getting a handle on using litestar, but I really like the framework and if there was someway to implement this with the after_request, that would be extremely helpful as easy for our team to maintain.

Thanks😄
questions.reference_answer:
You won't have much luck with a middleware, since the response body has already been serialised. 

There are several ways you could do this. One would be to implement a [custom response class](https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/responses.html#custom-responses):

```python
class ValidatingResponse[T](Response[T]):
    async def to_asgi_response[UserT, AuthT](self, request: Request[UserT, AuthT, Any]) -> ASGIResponse:
        content = self.content  # the unserialised response content
        user = request.user  # the user set by your auth middleware
        auth = request.auth  # the token instance created by your auth middleware


@get("/", response_class=ValidatingResponse)
async def get_things() -> Things:
    things = await fetch_things_from_db()
    return things
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete technical question with substantive answer; chunks [4] and [6] support the reasoning that Response/ASGIResponse architecture enables custom response classes and that after_response hooks execute too late for payload validation.

questions.title: Is 'transaction' reserved?
questions.question:
Is 'transaction' reserved?

I am following: https://docs.litestar.dev/2/tutorials/sqlalchemy/1-provide-session-with-di.html
Where I defined something like:
```python

@get("/sqlite")
async def get_list(transaction:AsyncSession) -> RecordCollection | dict[str, str]:
    logger.info("Access to get_list route detected.")

    results = [serialize_record(todo) for todo in await get_all_records(transaction)]
    if not results:
        return {"success": "No records found."}
    return results

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[index, upload_file, log_file, get_list],
               logging_config=logging_config,
               lifespan=[db_connection],
               dependencies={"transaction":provide_transaction}
               )
```

This runs fine, however, if I rename "transaction" into something like "active_session" the program fails.
So is `transaction` reserved for litestar?
questions.reference_answer: `transaction` is defined as a dependency (`dependencies={"transaction":provide_transaction}`). It will be injected under the key it's defined at. If you want to use a different key, you'll have to rename it in the dependency declaration as well as the function where it's being used.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [0] from the referenced tutorial shows how 'transaction' is declared as a named dependency, and chunk [3] demonstrates the DI pattern where dependency keys must match parameter names in handlers.

questions.title: How to use Background Tasks?
questions.question:
How to use Background Tasks?

Sorry, I’m just an AI engineer who has recently started trying to set up a server, so maybe I’ve asked a silly question. Please bear with me. 

I have an API that initiates a PyTorch training process based on the data sent from the frontend. Later, I realized that this locks up the backend, making it impossible for other APIs to be accessed. 

I looked into the official documentation, and it seems that using BackgroundTasks might be the correct solution? Like this? 

Is this the correct way to tackle long cpu-lock tasks?

```python
@post("/upload")
async def upload_file(
        data: UploadFile = Body(media_type=RequestEncodingType.MULTI_PART, default=None)
) -> Response[dict[str, str]]:
 
        return Response({
            "status": "success",
        }, background=BackgroundTask(train, max_epochs=10000, data_path=cleaned_file_path))

```

I’d also like to ask for another suggestion. On my server, I first preprocess the data and then run the training. From this perspective, the preprocessing part should also be implemented as a Background Task. 

I can split it into two APIs, where the frontend controls when to preprocess and when to train—I understand that concept.   

Alternatively, I could use a single API with two background tasks, passing them as a list. (If my understanding of Background is correct) That seems feasible to me as well.   

My question is: Can I directly call one API from another API? Like this:

```python

@post("/a")
async def a()

@post("/b")
async def b()
    # call a here
```

Is above code a silly choice?
questions.reference_answer:
> Is this the correct way to tackle long cpu-lock tasks?

No, it is not. I recommend reading this article from our docs: https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/topics/sync-vs-async

The background tasks still run in the same process as your application, so for CPU bound tasks, you're not gaining anything.

For long running / compute heavy tasks, you want to offload the process to a task queue, like [celery](https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/stable/index.html).
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about BackgroundTask suitability for CPU-bound tasks with substantive answer; chunks [0] and [4] from sync-vs-async docs directly support the explanation that CPU-bound operations have different requirements than background tasks.

questions.title: How to mock dependencies?
questions.question:
How to mock dependencies?

### Summary

Can you please add a dependency override to the application. Something similar to fastapi's app.dependency_overrides.
This is important when trying to override a dependency while writing tests.

### Basic Example

```python
@pytest.fixture
def client(app):
    database = TestAsyncSessionLocal()
    def test_get_db(app):
        try:
            yield database
        finally:
            database.close()
    app.dependency_overrides['db'] = Provide(test_get_db) # nothing like dependency_overrides in starlite
    ...
```

### Drawbacks and Impact

Here's what I tried:
```python
def client(app):
    database = TestAsyncSessionLocal()
    def test_get_db(app):
        try:
            yield database
        finally:
            database.close()
    app.dependencies['db'] = Provide(test_get_db)
    ...
```
This doesn't work because handlers are already registered in main:app. So overriding dependencies like this after handlers have been registered changes nothing. 

### Unresolved questions

_No response_
questions.reference_answer:
A couple of things here, but let me just say that this isn't something Litestar specific but rather about how scopes, imports and mocking works.

1. You are misusing the application factory pattern. Having a `create_app` function and then immediately calling it in the same file is functionally equivalent to simply creating the app in the file directly.
2. The issue you're seeing is because of the order of execution. What happens in your code is:
  1. You import `app`. This will also set the dependencies on that object
  2. You mock `app.core.database.get_db`. Since the application object has already been created, this will not change the `get_db` function you passed to it

To fix it you can either:

1. Mock `get_db` before you import `app` (requires the least amount of effort from your side)
2. Fix your broken patterns by not calling `create_app` immediately and instead importing it and calling it *after* you mocked `get_db`

The second approach is the preferred one and one of the reasons we don't provide dependency overrides: It encourages bad practices. As you've seen, the fact that Litestar does not provide you with such capabilities has brought to light a deeper issue in your code. 

## Examples

Here are two simplified examples that you can apply to your existing code:

### Importing the `app` locally

`dependencies.py`

```python
def some_dependency() -> str:
  return "foo"
```

`main.py`

```python
from starlite import Starlite, Provide, get
from dependencies import some_dependency


@get()
def handler(dep: str) -> str:
  return dep


app = Starlite([handler], dependencies={"dep": Provide(some_dependency)})
``` 

`test.py`

```python
from starlite import TestClient


def new_dependency():
    return "bar"


def test(mocker):
    mocker.patch("dependencies.some_dependency", new=new_dependency)
    from main import app

    with TestClient(app) as client:
        assert client.get("/").text == "bar"
``` 


### Using an application factory

`dependencies.py`

```python
def some_dependency() -> str:
  return "foo"
```

`main.py`

```python
from starlite import Starlite, Provide, get
from dependencies import some_dependency


@get()
def handler(dep: str) -> str:
  return dep

def create_app() -> Starlite:
  return Starlite([handler], dependencies={"dep": Provide(some_dependency)})
``` 

`test.py`

```python
from starlite import TestClient
from main import create_app
import pytest


def new_dependency():
    return "bar"

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def mock_db():
    return mocker.patch("dependencies.some_dependency", new=new_dependency)


@pytest.fixture
def app():
  return create_app()


def test(app):
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        assert client.get("/").text == "bar"
```
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete Litestar usage question with a substantive answer; chunk [5] directly addresses why Litestar lacks dependency_overrides and provides the recommended alternative strategies.

questions.title: redirect to different route_handler from a route_handler
questions.question:
redirect to different route_handler from a route_handler

Often i find myself after processing some user-request that i want to return a different page
than the one the user is on right now.

for example, after editing a user  (/usermanagement/{uid:int}/edit ) i want to redirect to the usermanagement-index-page, which i can do with a redirect of course... 

However - i'd like to "internally" reference the other routehandler and spare myself the unnecessary overhead of redirecting, and making the user retrieve more files than necessary again to load the whole page.. (especially since im using htmx to only return the necessary bits and pieces.)

Is there some internal_redirect function which i just overlooked ? Before i just added some route_handler_cb-function which i referenced then from all http_route handlers, but this is very repetetive and every call needs to be adapted to changes introduced by dependency-injections or other parameters..

Anyone can point me in the right direction ?
questions.reference_answer: There's no "internal redirect", but you can use [`app.route_reverse`](https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/reference/app.html#litestar.app.Litestar.route_reverse) to retrieve the path for a route handler by its name, and supply that to your redirect.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks 0 and 1 directly document the route_reverse API solution, while chunk 4 provides additional details about its behavior with multiple route paths.

questions.title: CORS for Router or Controller
questions.question:
CORS for Router or Controller

Hi I'm creating an application using Litestar and I noticed that in the documentation is mentioned that is possible to add CORSMiddleware to Litestar app instance. Is it possible to possible to pass CORSConfig or CORSMiddleware to a Router or a Controller? Or do I have to create an "ad hoc" Middleware?
questions.reference_answer: Yes, it is. All layers (application, routers, controllers, handlers) accept `middleware`. You can read more about this here: https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/applications.html#layered-architecture
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [3] explicitly states that middleware can be configured on any layer in Litestar's layered architecture, and chunk [2] demonstrates middleware applied at the route handler level, both directly supporting the answer.

questions.title: How to inject a dependency in a Controller class?
questions.reference_answer:
@l3t2st1r I'm sorry if I didn't understand your request well (I'm also not a Litestar expert and I don't know if this is a good approach), but you can use `super()` to use the argument of the parent controller in the child controllers. Something like this

<details>
<summary>Example code</summary>

```python
from litestar import Litestar, Controller, get
from litestar.di import Provide


async def str_fn() -> str:
    return "Dependency example"


class BaseController(Controller):
    path = "/"
    dependencies = {"db_handler": Provide(str_fn)}

    @get("parent")
    async def parent_get(self) -> str:
        return "Parent controller"


class ChildController(BaseController):
    path = "/child/"

    def __init__(self, owner):
        super().__init__(owner)

    @get("child")
    async def child_get(self) -> str:
        # parent dependencies using super()
        parent_dependencies = self.dependencies.get("db_handler")
        # prints Dependency example
        print(await parent_dependencies.dependency())
        return "Child controller"


class OtherController(BaseController):
    path = "/other/"

    def __init__(self, owner):
        super().__init__(owner)

    @get("other")
    async def other_get(self) -> str:
        # parent dependencies using super()
        parent_dependencies = self.dependencies.get("db_handler")
        # prints Dependency example
        print(await parent_dependencies.dependency())
        return "Other controller"


app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[
        BaseController,
        ChildController,
        OtherController,
    ]
)
```
</details>

Again, sorry if I missed your point.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks demonstrate the exact pattern for declaring dependencies on Controller classes using `dependencies = {"key": Provide(function)}`, which is the core mechanism shown in the answer.

questions.title: Lots of idle sessions with sqlalchemy+psycopg
questions.question:
Lots of idle sessions with sqlalchemy+psycopg

Moving a question I had on discord to here @cofin 

Some version information:
Python 3.11
Litestar 2.9.0
psycopg 3.1.18
uvicorn 0.30.3
Postgres 16

I did some load testing for my litestar application (2 uvicorn workers) and realized that I would hit the postgres connection limit relatively easily. This is set to 100 by default, but what's confusing to me is that the engine default settings should not even allow 50 connections in the first place.

I tested multiple configurations, but the one below I ended on. For load testing I use locust with 10 users and 0.5 user/s ramp up. The test runs for 1 minute. The test is simple: log in and then just query 1 endpoint. What I would expect is to see a sudden ramp up to 10-20 sessions, then between 10-20 sessions during the test and at the end returning to 10 idle sessions (2x pool size). I appended a snippet from pg admin below - instead the idle sessions ramp up to 61. And stay there after the test. 

Before I simplified the locust test, the session behavior was a bit more "oscillating", e.g., 22, 54, 35, 54, 44, 80, 42, 96, 45, 55, 48. (see second screenshot)
So, something is cleaning up those sessions eventually - just not how I thought it would.

Unfortunately I cannot recreate the problem with a simple example. 

```python
    engine_config = EngineConfig(
        pool_pre_ping=True,
        pool_size=5,
        # pool_recycle=3600,
        max_overflow=5,
        pool_use_lifo=True,
        connect_args={
            "application_name": f"LITESTAR-{os.getpid()}"
        }
    )
    session_config = AsyncSessionConfig(expire_on_commit=False)
    sqlalchemy_config = SQLAlchemyAsyncConfig(
        connection_string=conn or get_connection_string(),
        session_config=session_config,
        engine_config=engine_config
    )  # Create 'async_session' dependency.
    # This will take care of disposing the async engine at the end of the app lifespan
    sqlalchemy_init_plugin = SQLAlchemyInitPlugin(config=sqlalchemy_config)
```

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c4c21969-b383-44c2-a842-ec21b3a68ff2)

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2857bdc6-83a4-45b3-9ae4-3774dc74727b)
questions.reference_answer:
And that is exactly the issue - instead of fetching the session maker from the plugin I implicitly create a new engine per session maker. Instead, I should get the session maker like
```python
async def retrieve_user_handler(session: dict[str, Any], connection: ASGIConnection) -> User:
    """Fetches the current user state from the database and returns it as User instance."""
    sessionmaker: Callable[[], AsyncSession] = connection.app.state.get("session_maker_class")
    db_session = sessionmaker()
```

With this, I don't even exhaust the base pool of connections.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete SQLAlchemy configuration issue with a substantive answer explaining improper session maker retrieval; chunks [0] and [7] directly address the correct pattern of using the plugin's dependency injection and app.state management.

questions.title: How do I generate typescript types for a recursive data type?
questions.question:
How do I generate typescript types for a recursive data type?

The following class causes issues when generating typescript types:

```py
@dataclass
class Prefix:
    path: str
    is_special: bool
    name: str = field(init=False)
    parent: str | None = field(init=False)
    children: list["Prefix"] = field(default_factory=list)

    def __post_init__(self):
        path = self.path.rsplit("/", maxsplit=1)
        self.name = path[1] if len(path) > 1 else path[0]
        self.parent = None if len(path) == 1 else path[0]
```

I get the error

> RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded

As you can see, the `Prefix.children` property is a `list[Prefix]`, which causes the recursion to continue indefinitely. How can I fix this?

questions.title: Creating grouped SQLAlchemyDTO return object
questions.question:
Creating grouped SQLAlchemyDTO return object

Hello,

I'm finally upgrading some code from starlite -> litestar and I'm having an issue with a "grouped" object from old starlite code with the new SQLAlchemyDTO:

```
class Foo(Base):
    __tablename__ = "foo"
    id: Mapped[String] = mapped_column(String, primary_key=True)

FooDTO = SQLAlchemyDTO[Foo]

class Bar(BaseModel):
    class Config:
        arbitrary_types_allowed = True
    foos: list[Foo]
    blah: str

@get("/get_bar", dto=FooDTO)
async def get_bar(db_session: AsyncSession) -> Bar:
    foos = await db_session.scalars(select(Foo))
    return Bar(foos=foos.all(), blah="hello")
```

This complains that the response cannot be serialised because Foo isn't serlializable, which makes sense as that's what the DTO is for. If I swap the type to be `foos: list[FooDTO]` it then fails pydantic validation because it is obviously the wrong type.

I also had to add an arbitrary_types_allowed which makes me believe I'm on the wrong track... 

How would I achieve this in litestar? Before it would just magically convert using the dto_factory.

Tested on a clean env 

[app.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/15914471/app.txt)
[pip_freeze.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/15914474/pip_freeze.txt)


Edit: Is there a way to convert in the route to get an instance of FooDTO from an instance of Foo so I can directly create a Bar?
questions.reference_answer:
I think you're looking for the pattern documented here: https://docs.litestar.dev/2/usage/dto/1-abstract-dto.html#wrapping-return-data

```
from typing import Generic

class Foo(Base):
    __tablename__ = "foo"
    id: Mapped[String] = mapped_column(String, primary_key=True)

FooDTO = SQLAlchemyDTO[Foo]

T = TypeVar("T")

@dataclass
class Bar(Generic[T]):

    foos: list[T]
    blah: str

@get("/get_bar", dto=FooDTO)
async def get_bar(db_session: AsyncSession) -> Bar[Foo]:
    foos = await db_session.scalars(select(Foo))
    return Bar(foos=foos.all(), blah="hello")
```
questions.curate_reason: Changelog entry [2] directly explains the generic wrapper type pattern with supported type arguments used in the accepted answer.

questions.title: Question: How to make litestar apply msgspec custom enc_hook / dec_hook functions to serialize/deserialize custom types?
questions.question:
Question: How to make litestar apply msgspec custom enc_hook / dec_hook functions to serialize/deserialize custom types?

Using msgspec for modelling my entities, I need to control serialization/deserialization for some custom types. Msgspec provides a mechanism for this, which involves writing custom enc_hook / dec_hook functions (see [msgspec extending docs](https://jcristharif.com/msgspec/extending.html)), which then can be passed to the encoding/decoding methods of msgspec.

My question is: When I now want to use these models in litestar, where / how can I tell litestar to use my custom enc_hook / dec_hook functions?
questions.reference_answer:
You can pass `type_encoders` / `type_decoders` on every layer. These allow you to define custom msgspec hooks.

https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/reference/app.html#litestar.app.Litestar.params.type_encoders
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete, valid question about Litestar's configuration API for custom type handling, and chunks 0 and 2 from docs/usage/custom-types.rst directly document the type_encoders/type_decoders mechanism with examples.

questions.title: How to achieve multiple authetication middlewares
questions.question:
How to achieve multiple authetication middlewares

I am doing server side session authentication. I have 2 groups of users - "users" / administrators and customers. A person may be an administrator and user same time. So these are 2 different entities. 
I want to have sessions of users in 1 folder, and sessions of final customers in the other. 
So 1 "app" needs to operate on e.g. usual "session" cookie and a folder called "user_sessions".
Another "app" needs to operate on e.g. "customer_session" cookie and a folder called "customer_sessions".
What building blocks can help to achieve this?
questions.reference_answer:
I think the following acts as I expect, although I am not sure if there is a better way or if I do the thing "by the book".

```
from typing import Any, Dict, Optional

import msgspec
from typing_extensions import TypeAlias

from litestar.stores.file import FileStore
from litestar import Litestar, Request, get, post, Controller
from litestar.connection import ASGIConnection
from litestar.dto import DTOData, MsgspecDTO
from litestar.exceptions import NotAuthorizedException
from litestar.middleware.session.server_side import ServerSideSessionBackend, ServerSideSessionConfig
from litestar import  Router

from litestar.security.session_auth import SessionAuth
from litestar.testing import TestClient

AnyConnection: TypeAlias = ASGIConnection[Any, Any, Any, Any]


class Customer(msgspec.Struct):
    customer_id: str


class Business(msgspec.Struct):
    business_id: str



MOCK_DB_CUSTOMERS: Dict[str, Customer] = {
    "1": Customer('1')
}

MOCK_DB_BUSINESS: Dict[str, Business] = {
    "A": Business('A')
}



async def retrieve_customer_handler(session: Dict[str, Any], _: AnyConnection) -> Optional[Customer]:
    return MOCK_DB_CUSTOMERS.get(session.get("customer_id") or "")


async def retrieve_business_handler(session: Dict[str, Any], _: AnyConnection) -> Optional[Business]:
    return MOCK_DB_BUSINESS.get(session.get("business_id") or "")



customer_session_auth = SessionAuth[Customer, ServerSideSessionBackend](
    retrieve_user_handler=retrieve_customer_handler,
    session_backend_config=ServerSideSessionConfig(store='customer_sessions', key='customer_session'),
)
business_session_auth = SessionAuth[Customer, ServerSideSessionBackend](
    retrieve_user_handler=retrieve_business_handler,
    session_backend_config=ServerSideSessionConfig(store='business_sessions', key='business_session'),
)


class CustomerController(Controller):
    middleware = [customer_session_auth.middleware]
    
    @get('/')
    def handler(self, request: Request) -> None:
        return None


    @post("/login_customer", dto=MsgspecDTO[Customer], exclude_from_auth=True)
    async def login_customer(self, data: DTOData[Customer], request: Request[Any, Any, Any]) -> Customer:
        # I skipped locating the customer and actual auth stuff, just provide an id of user to login (example only)
        customer_id = data.as_builtins()['customer_id']
        try:
            customer = MOCK_DB_CUSTOMERS[customer_id]
        except KeyError as e:
            raise NotAuthorizedException from e

        request.set_session({"customer_id": customer_id})
        return customer



class BusinessController(Controller):
    middleware = [business_session_auth.middleware]
    
    @get('/', sync_to_thread=False)
    def handler(self, request: Request) -> None:
        return None

    @post("/login_business", dto=MsgspecDTO[Business], exclude_from_auth=True)
    async def login_business(self, data: DTOData[Business], request: Request[Any, Any, Any]) -> Business:
        # I skipped locating the business and actual auth stuff, just provide an id of user to login (example only)
        business_id = data.as_builtins()['business_id']
        try:
            business = MOCK_DB_BUSINESS[business_id]
        except KeyError as e:
            raise NotAuthorizedException from e

        request.set_session({"business_id": business_id})
        return business


app = Litestar(
    route_handlers=[
        Router('/b', route_handlers=[BusinessController]),
        Router('/c', route_handlers=[CustomerController]),
    ],
    # on_app_init=[session_auth.on_app_init],
    openapi_config=None,
    debug=True,
    stores={
        "business_sessions": FileStore('/tmp/business_sessions'),
        "customer_sessions": FileStore('/tmp/customer_sessions')
    }
)


def test_no_user() -> None:
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response = client.get("/c/")
        assert response.status_code == 401

        response = client.get("/b/")
        assert response.status_code == 401


def test_business_only_user() -> None:
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response = client.post("/b/login_business", json={"business_id": "A"})
        assert response.status_code == 201

        response = client.get("/b")
        assert response.status_code == 200

        response = client.get("/c")
        assert response.status_code == 401


def test_customer_only_user() -> None:
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response = client.post("/c/login_customer", json={"customer_id": "1"})
        assert response.status_code == 201

        response = client.get("/b")
        assert response.status_code == 401

        response = client.get("/c")
        assert response.status_code == 200

def test_business_and_customer_user() -> None:
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response = client.post("/b/login_business", json={"business_id": "A"})
        assert response.status_code == 201
        
        response = client.post("/c/login_customer", json={"customer_id": "1"})
        assert response.status_code == 201

        response = client.get("/b")
        assert response.status_code == 200

        response = client.get("/c")
        assert response.status_code == 200
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about implementing multiple session auth middlewares answered with working code using SessionAuth instances with different ServerSideSessionConfig settings; chunks [1] and [5] document SessionAuth.middleware property and configuration pattern.

questions.title: How to add multiple auth middlewares?
questions.question: How to add multiple auth middlewares? ### Summary I'd love some documentation on how to chain together authentication middlewares until either one succeeds, and performs the request, or they all fail and the API returns a 401. My use case is is I'd like to use the JWT middleware to authenticate some requests, that come from users, from a bearer token, but then also a custom middleware to authenticate API requests from an X-API-Key header. I don't want API calls to 401 because the JWT middleware fails. I don't know if this is possible through configuration, or if I'd need to make an overarching middleware that somehow calls the others in tandem, but I couldn't find any documentation on how to achieve this. Apologies if it exists and I missed it. ## Funding * If you would like to see an issue prioritized, make a pledge towards it! * We receive the pledge once the issue is completed & verified
questions.reference_answer:
Middlewares already call other middlewares / ASGI apps, since they themselves are ASGI apps as well.

It usually looks something like 

```python
await self.app(scope, send, receive)
```

So if you were to wrap one middleware into another, you'd simply wrap these calls. 

You can take a look at https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/a288fd49ec50426da146f8e9071163d5b892c335/litestar/middleware/base.py or the middlewares in https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/tree/a288fd49ec50426da146f8e9071163d5b892c335/litestar/middleware to see how that's usually implemented.
questions.curate_reason: This is a concrete usage question with a substantive answer explaining the ASGI middleware composition pattern; chunks 2-3 directly show the `await app(scope, receive, send)` pattern referenced in the answer.

questions.title: HTMX and jinja templates in single app
questions.question:
HTMX and jinja templates in single app

I want some my endpoints to respond with full pages, while others to support partial content updates with htmx. 
As far as I understand `template_config` on application accepts only 1 template engine and I can not stack applications on each other.
questions.reference_answer:
You want regular, full Jinja routes and then partial HTMX routes?
You can see https://github.com/JacobCoffee/niapi/tree/main/app/domain/web/templates for a crude example that uses both
- https://github.com/JacobCoffee/niapi/blob/main/app/domain/calculator/controllers/calculator.py

Basically for your full templates you would just return a `Template` but for the partials you want to do via HTMX you would return an [HTMXTemplate](https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/htmx.html#htmxtemplate-response-classes)

HTMX is not its own templating engine, but can be used with any we provide or any custom ones (as you can see in my partial HTMX HTML file, it also uses Jinja to fill in some blanks)
questions.curate_reason: Chunks demonstrate the solution: configure a single Litestar app with one TemplateConfig and HTMXPlugin, then return either Template or HTMXTemplate responses from handlers.

questions.title: Access to app state in CLI plugins
questions.reference_answer:
`Litestar.lifespan()` is documented: https://docs.litestar.dev/2/reference/app.html#litestar.app.Litestar.lifespan

The lifespan is only entered by the application in response to the ASGI lifespan startup message from the server, which doesn't happen for cli operations (except `litestar run` of course).

Your approach looks reasonable to me. Are you encountering any issues?

Another way you can approach it is to have your own lifespan context that is separate from, but called from within the litestar lifespan context. This way you can enter resource contexts separate from the application.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question with a substantive answer confirming app.lifespan() is documented public API; chunks 1/2/6/8 cover accessing app in CLI, app.state, lifespan context managers, and CLIPlugin respectively.

questions.title: Upload zip or tar.gz file in POST
questions.question:
Upload zip or tar.gz file in POST

I have the following to be able to upload a .tar.gz file.
`@post("/upload", media_type="application/x-gtar")
    async def upload(self,
        data: Annotated[UploadFile, Body(media_type=RequestEncodingType.MULTI_PART)]
    ) -> dict[str, Any]:
        return { 
            "File Name": data.filename,
            "Content Type": data.content_type,
            "Headers": data.headers
        }`
However, I keep getting 500 internal server errors. Am I implementing this correctly?
questions.reference_answer:
`media_type` is used to serialize whatever you return from the handler `upload`. Litestar does not know how to serialize `dict[str, Any]` into a tar file which is why it fails.

`media_type` is not used to indicate that your route accepts tar gz files.

You can remove `media_type` and you will notice that you can indeed upload a file and get a json response
questions.curate_reason: Concrete API error question with substantive answer; chunk [0] directly explains media_type serializes responses, [1] and [3] show the correct multipart upload pattern using Body(media_type=) on parameters.

questions.title: 200 Response without body and content-type
questions.question:
200 Response without body and content-type

How can I make an endpoint return a Response without a body and without a content-type? Returning `None` from the route handler just gets me "application/json" media type with `null` as the body. I have looked in the documentation but I can't find anything.
questions.reference_answer:
"200 without a body" isn't really a thing. As @guacs said, if you truly want to omit the body, you should use a `204` code. However, if you're fine with sending an empty body, you could just do something like

```python
@get("/")
async def handler() -> str:
    return ""
```

which sends an empty plaintext response.
questions.curate_reason: Chunks [0] and [8] directly explain that 204 status code allows no response body and that content-type headers are omitted for such responses.

questions.question:

Hi.

questions.title: Get a route handler `name` in a template?
questions.question:
Get a route handler `name` in a template?

Would be great to get an access to a route name variable in a template instead of  pass it into a context every single time.

## What i do
Handler
```python
...
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
        return Template(template_name='login.html', context=dict(route_name='login'))
...
```

Template
```
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(route_name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

## What would be good
Template
```
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

Is possible to get an access to?
questions.reference_answer:
Heya, what you propose `url_for(request.route_handler.name)` already works inside a template. `request`  is passed by default into the template context.

Here is an example that uses HTMX and Jinja as two separate routes. The assert passes, meaning it does have access and is able to get the name.

```python
from litestar import get
from litestar.contrib.htmx.request import HTMXRequest
from litestar.contrib.htmx.response import HTMXTemplate
from litestar.contrib.jinja import JinjaTemplateEngine
from litestar.response import Template
from litestar.template.config import TemplateConfig
from litestar.testing import create_test_client


@get("hello_htmx", name="hello_htmx_route")
async def hello_htmx() -> HTMXTemplate:
    return HTMXTemplate(
        template_str="url is {{url_for(request.route_handler.name)}}", push_url="/form"
    )


@get("hello", name="hello_route")
async def hello() -> Template:
    return Template(template_str="url is {{url_for(request.route_handler.name)}}")


with create_test_client(
    [hello_htmx],
    request_class=HTMXRequest,
    template_config=TemplateConfig(directory=".", engine=JinjaTemplateEngine),
) as client:
    response = client.get("hello_htmx")
    assert response.text == "url is /hello_htmx"


with create_test_client(
    [hello],
    template_config=TemplateConfig(directory=".", engine=JinjaTemplateEngine),
) as client:
    response = client.get("hello")
    assert response.text == "url is /hello"
```

Now, if your question is to have a default name to the router itself, then it is an ongoing issue discussed over at https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/issues/2892
questions.curate_reason: Chunks [4] and [6] document the url_for method and its template-specific wrapper, which are directly used in the solution to access route handler names in templates.

questions.title: How to print all request headers?

```
{"status_code":400,"detail":"Validation failed for GET http://127.0.0.1:8000/","extra":[{"message":"Unsupported type: <class 'dict'>","key":"headers"}]}
```

I feel like I'm misunderstanding something. Can anyone point out what I'm doing wrong?

Also, the [Default signature namespace](https://docs.litestar.dev/2/usage/routing/handlers.html#default-signature-namespace) section of the docs mentions that I can place the `Headers` import inside an `if TYPE_CHECKING:` block. But when I do this:

```

@get("/", sync_to_thread=False)
def index(headers: Headers) -> str:

```
... I get the following error when I try to run the app:

```
                       ^^^^^^^
NameError: name 'Headers' is not defined
```

questions.reference_answer: Hi @elliotwaite, unfortunately, you've come across a bug that can't be fixed until version 3 because it would constitute a breaking change; The headers injected into the route handler via the `headers` parameter isn't actually a `Headers` instance but a `dict`. For now, if you need access to a `Headers` instance, you'll have to use the method @Alc-Alc provided. If you're fine with a dict, you can just type `headers` as a dict.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [5] directly shows the correct way to declare request headers as Dict[str, str] in route handlers, which is the practical solution explained in the accepted answer for working around the headers-injection bug.

questions.title: 400: JSON is malformed: invalid character when trying to send binary data
questions.question: 400: JSON is malformed: invalid character when trying to send binary data ### Description Hello, I have a client that sends a pytorch model (after converting it to base64 string), for which I want my litestar server to read it and use it afterwards. My client code: ``` import io import torch import base64 import requests model = torch.hub.load('pytorch/vision:v0.10.0', 'alexnet', pretrained=True) buffer = io.BytesIO() torch.save(model, buffer) buffer.seek(0) model = buffer.read() model = base64.b64encode(model).decode() data = {"module_name": "module", "version_name": "version", "model": model} response = requests.post("http://localhost:8080/api/v1/register", data=data) print(response) ``` My server code: ``` from typing import Annotated from dataclasses import dataclass import io import torch import mlflow import mlflow.pytorch from litestar import Controller, post @dataclass class RegisterRequest: module_name: str version_name: str model: str class MLFlowController(Controller): @post("/register") async def register(self, data: RegisterRequest) -> None: model = data.model # model = base64.b64decode(model) # model = torch.load(io.BytesIO(model)) # mlflow.pytorch.log_model(model, f"{data.module_name}_{data.version_name}") ``` However, my litestar server is throwing the following error: `litestar.exceptions.http_exceptions.ClientException: 400: JSON is malformed: invalid character (byte 0)` I tried using dict[str, str] instead of creating a dataclass but it did not help. What should I do for my scenario? Note that sending a pytorch model is just an example; I might send a tensor or anything else that is not a simple class. Also I do not want to use multi-part requests for infra reasons). ### URL to code causing the issue _No response_ ### MCVE _No response_ ### Steps to reproduce _No response_ ### Screenshots _No response_ ### Logs _No response_ ### Litestar Version 2 (latest) ### Platform - [X] Linux - [ ] Mac - [ ] Windows - [ ] Other (Please specify in the description above) --- > [!NOTE] > While we are open for sponsoring on [GitHub Sponsors](https://github.com/sponsors/litestar-org/) and > [OpenCollective](https://opencollective.com/litestar), we also utilize [Polar.sh](https://polar.sh/) to engage in pledge-based sponsorship. > > Check out all issues funded or available for funding [on our Polar.sh Litestar dashboard](https://polar.sh/litestar-org) > * If you would like to see an issue prioritized, make a pledge towards it! > * We receive the pledge once the issue is completed & verified > * This, along with engagement in the community, helps us know which features are a priority to our users.
questions.reference_answer:
> Also I do not want to use multi-part requests for infra reasons).

But you are. Passing a dict to `data` will cause it to be form encoded. 

From the requests docs:

> `data` – the body to attach to the request. If a dictionary or list of tuples `[(key, value)] is provided`, form-encoding will take place.

https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/#requests.Request

The error you are seeing is because you are telling Litestar to decode form encoded data as JSON.

You need to either send your data as JSON (e.g. by encoding it as a string before passing it to the `data` parameter, or passing your dictionary to the `json` parameter instead), or by setting the appropriate content-type in your handler so Litestar can decode it correctly: https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/requests.html#content-type

questions.title: Raising HTTPException vs returning Response
questions.question:
Raising HTTPException vs returning Response

I'm trying to understand the difference between raising an `HTTPException` vs returning a `Response`. Consider the following example:

```py
from typing import Any

from litestar import Litestar, Response, get
from litestar.exceptions import HTTPException
from litestar.status_codes import HTTP_402_PAYMENT_REQUIRED
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Character(BaseModel):
    name: str
    nickname: str | None = None


the_dude = Character(name="Jeffrey Lebowski", nickname="The Dude")


@get("/response")
async def response(extra: bool = False) -> Response[dict[str, Any]]:
    return Response(
        status_code=HTTP_402_PAYMENT_REQUIRED,
        content=dict(
            status_code=HTTP_402_PAYMENT_REQUIRED,
            detail="Where's the money, Lebowski?",
            extra=the_dude if extra else {},
        ),
    )


@get("/exception")
async def exception(extra: bool = False) -> None:
    raise HTTPException(
        status_code=HTTP_402_PAYMENT_REQUIRED,
        detail="Where's the money, Lebowski?",
        extra=the_dude if extra else {},
    )


app = Litestar(route_handlers=[response, exception])
```

1. `GET /response` and `GET /exception` return the same status and body. In what case would you use the one or the other, or it's just a matter of style?
2. `GET /response?extra=1` works, `GET /exception?extra=1` raises `SerializationException: Unsupported type: <class 'foo.Character'>`. Is it deliberate that `Response` knows how to serialize Pydantic models and HTTPException doesn't? If yes why? If not I'd suggest this as a feature request.
questions.reference_answer:
>  In what case would you use the one or the other, or it's just a matter of style?

In this example, they are functionally equivalent. The difference is mainly control flow. You can easily raise an `HTTPException` somewhere deep within a nested call you made from your route handler, and it will immediately abort the request. To do this with a regular `Response`, you'd have to explicitly pass that as a return type up the chain.

> Is it deliberate that `Response` knows how to serialize Pydantic models and HTTPException doesn't? 

It is, yes. Exceptions are intentionally more "low level" than responses, simply to avoid complexity and a whole catalogue of potential exception cases within those exceptions. The closer exceptions come to regular responses, the more can go wrong with them.

That being said, we *could* support this, but I'd argue that it should be opt-in, e.g. with a special `ExceptionResponse` that you can `raise`, but will go the same path as the regular responses. I'd like to see some use cases for this though before we go ahead and add this feature.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question about HTTPException vs Response control flow; chunks [2] and [5] directly explain the different exception handling paths versus response returns.

questions.title: Custom dependency injection mechanism
questions.reference_answer:
How about something like this?

```python
from typing import AsyncIterator

from dependency_injector import containers, providers

from starlite import Provide, Starlite, State, get


# cache.py
class Cache:
    @classmethod
    async def from_url(cls) -> "Cache":
        return cls()

    async def get(self) -> None:
        print("Value")


async def init_cache() -> AsyncIterator[Cache]:
    cache = await Cache.from_url()
    yield cache


# containers.py
class Container(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    cache = providers.Resource(init_cache)


# controllers.py
@get("/1")
async def controller(cache: Cache) -> None:
    await cache.get()  # >>> Value


# main.py
async def get_cache(state: State) -> Cache:
    return await state.container.cache()


async def on_startup(state: State) -> None:
    # XXX: can't pass `container.init_resources()` directly into `Starlite.on_startup` because of AnyIO worker thread
    container = Container()
    container.wire(modules=[__name__])
    await container.init_resources()
    state.container = container


def create_app() -> Starlite:
    app = Starlite(
        route_handlers=[controller],
        dependencies={"cache": Provide(get_cache)},
        on_startup=[on_startup],
    )
    return app
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete question about integrating external DI library with Litestar; accepted answer shows valid pattern using Provide() with callable providers, directly supported by documentation on dependency declaration syntax and callable provider support.

questions.title: How to share a httpx.AsyncClient() across the app?
questions.question:
How to share a httpx.AsyncClient() across the app?

I need to use httpx.AsyncClient() and I've read in their docs it's more optimal to have a single client that you share across the app vs creating a client each time in the route handler.

How can I share this client so that is available across all routes and then I can safely close it on app shutdown?
questions.reference_answer:
> and I've read in their docs it's more optimal to have a single client that you share across the app

I am not aware of this. However, there's nothing preventing you from doing that. You can just create an instance of the client and then import that.

If you want something that's specifically bound to the lifetime of the application, you could of course use application state, dependency injection and the lifetime hooks.

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.datastructures import State
from litestar.di import Provide
from httpx import AsyncClient


def on_startup(state: State) -> None:
  state.client = AsyncClient()


async def on_shutdown(state: State) -> None:
  await state.client.aclose()


def get_client(state: State) -> AsyncClient:
  return state.client


app = Litestar(
  on_startup=[on_startup],
  on_shutdown=[on_shutdown], 
  dependencies={"client": Provide(get_client)}
)
```
questions.curate_reason: Chunks document State management in on_startup and dependency injection with Provide, the core patterns used in the answer.

questions.title: How to define "endpoint" without using "decorator syntax" ?
questions.question:
How to define "endpoint" without using "decorator syntax" ?

An starlette example,

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Starlette(routes=[
        Mount(root_path, routes=[
            Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET','POST'], name='mail'),
        ])
    ]
)
```

This way we can have central control on the route connections and easy to configure.
questions.reference_answer:
The issue you're having is that you want to do this

> This way we can have central control on the route connections and easy to configure.

but arrived at the conclusion that the way to achieve it is to not use decorators.

This conclusion however is wrong, since what you want is already how Litestar works.

The equivalent of this Starlette app

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Starlette(routes=[Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET'], name='mail')])
```

is this Litestar app:

```python
@get("/{token:uuid}", name="mail")
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[mail_page])
```

So to answer your question: You can't. Decorators are how you define the route handlers. 
But you also don't *need* to, since the decorators are not registering the route handlers at the same time. These are strictly separated in Litestar.

The sanic example you provided is *not* equivalent to what's happening in Litestar.

```python
@app.route("/test", methods=["GET")
async def handler(request):
  ...
```

in this Sanic example, `@app.route` does two things: It creates an endpoint function, and *registers it on the application*, which, going off your initial post here is what you want avoid. But this is not what's happening with Litestar's decorators. Notice how in the Sanic example `.route` is a **method on the application instance**. In Litestar, this is not the case. Decorating a function with a route handler decorator such as `@post` does not do anything besides creating a route handler. It does not register it, since there's no application associated with it.


You can even make the Litestar and Starlette equivalents look almost identical if you really want to:

```python
async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

mail_page = Route(path='/{token:uuid}', endpoint=mail_page, methods=['GET'], name='mail')

app = Starlette(routes=[mail_page])
```

is this Litestar app:

```python

async def mail_page(request: Request):
    ...

mail_page = get("/{token:uuid}", name="mail")(mail_page)

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[mail_page])
```

It's simply a difference in syntax, not in functionality.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete, valid question about Litestar's route definition API with a substantive architectural explanation; chunks [0], [1], [4] directly demonstrate and support the key answer point that decoration and registration are strictly separated in Litestar.

questions.title: How do you handle settings?
questions.question:
How do you handle settings?

How do you handle loading settings from ENV and then making them available in the route handlers?

At the moment I'm using a BaseSettings class from pydantic. Is this the right way? And how do I make this available in all the route handlers that need it?
questions.reference_answer:
We have created an `AppSettings` class based off `BaseSettings`.  We then use dependency injection to make the settings available.

*`config.py`*
```python
class AppSettings(BaseSettings):
    app_name: str = "My Awesome App"
    app_short_name: str = "awesome"
    admin_email: str = "foo@bar.com"
    mongodb_url: str
    mongodb_db: str
```

*`dependencies.py`*
```python
@lru_cache()
def get_settings() -> AppSettings:
    return AppSettings()
```

*`main.py`*
```python
app = Starlite(
    ...
    dependencies={
        "settings": Provide(get_settings, use_cache=True),
    },
)
```

Within a given controller, we can say something like:

```python
async def my_endpoint(
    self,
    settings: AppSettings,
)
```

...and the `settings` variable holds our app settings.

Since this is based off Pydantic's `BaseSettings` we can use a mixture of default / hard-coded, environment variables, and `.env` files.

https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/dependency-injection.html

https://docs.pydantic.dev/usage/settings/
questions.curate_reason: The answer demonstrates using Litestar's dependency injection with Provide() and use_cache at the app level to inject settings into handlers, which is directly shown in chunks [0] and [3].

questions.title: Changing operation names for handlers in the schema
questions.question:
Changing operation names for handlers in the schema

Maybe I am looking in the wrong place for this functionality. By default, HTTP-method decorated route handlers are given title-case converted names in the schema documentation. For some handlers, the title casing is not ideal, or otherwise one might want a name that is not necessarily the same as the name of the function.

My expectation would be that the name parameter passed to the decorator would override the default name, but that does not seem to be the case. Is there a way to change the operation name in the docs for an operation associated with a handler?

e.g., I am expecting this would override "Urls" in the docs to being "URLs":

```
@get(path="/urls", name="URLs")

```
questions.reference_answer:
Oh, I found it. The parameter is `operation_id`. So, for future reference, this should work:

```

```

questions.title: How do you get access to the cache from an JWTAuthenticationMiddleware?
questions.question:
How do you get access to the cache from an JWTAuthenticationMiddleware?

In the docs I see it being accessed using `scope["app"].cache` but the authenticate_request of JWTAuthenticationMiddleware does not have a scope param?
questions.reference_answer:
> but the authenticate_request of JWTAuthenticationMiddleware does not have a scope param?

`scope` is an attribute of `ASGIConnection`, which is passed into `authenticate_request`.
So you could access the scope like this: `connection.scope`, and get the cache with `connection.scope["app"].cache`.

You don't need to go through so many indirections though, as `ASGIConenction` directly exposes `app` and `cache` properties, which means the easiest way to access it is `connection.cache`.

Check out the [`ASGIConnection` docs](https://starliteproject.dev/lib/reference/connection/base.html#starlite.connection.base.ASGIConnection).
questions.curate_reason: Concrete API usage question with substantive answer; chunks [0] and [3] document the authenticate_request(connection) method signature and ASGIConnection API that the answer references.

questions.title: Returning a Pillow image that only exists in memory
questions.question:
Returning a Pillow image that only exists in memory

I have a program that programmatically creates an image using Pillow. I want to return it using Starlite. I tried combining a bit FastAPI-inspired code from Stackoverflow + following the docs a bit, but couldn't get it to work.

```python
@get(
    "randompic/{lang:str}",
    media_type='image/jpeg'
)
def randompic(lang: str) -> io.BytesIO:
    img = rdpgen.generate_random_definition_pic(lang)
    # return img as bytes
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buf, format="JPEG")
    buf.seek(0)

    return Response(content=buf, media_type="image/jpeg")

```
If you send a request, then you get the response `{"status_code":500,"detail":"Unable to serialize response content"}`. What would be the best way to achieve this?
questions.reference_answer:
Two things:

1. Your return annotation is incorrect. You are not returning `io.BytesIO` but a `Response`
2. If you want to send a "raw" response, you should provide bytes, not `io.BytesIO`.

Therefore, the fix looks like this:

```python
@get(
    "randompic/{lang:str}",
    media_type='image/jpeg'
)
def randompic(lang: str) -> Response[bytes]:
    img = rdpgen.generate_random_definition_pic(lang)
    # return img as bytes
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    img.save(buf, format="JPEG")
    buf.seek(0)

    return Response(content=buf.read(), media_type="image/jpeg")
```

Alternatively you could do what @Goldziher suggested and stream the file from memory, which depending on its size might be an advantage or a disadvantage.
questions.curate_reason: Chunk [5] directly supports the answer by explaining that 'the handler's return annotation drives serialization,' which is the core principle underlying the fix.

questions.title: How To Handle Path Parameters of Multiple Valid Types
questions.question:
How To Handle Path Parameters of Multiple Valid Types

We have endpoints where a path parameter can be multiple types (string, integer, boolean).  These are normally used for filtering.  For example:

```
class Widget(Document):
    name: str
    some_bool: bool
    some_str: str
    some_int: int


class WidgetByAttr(str, Enum):
    # Set of widget attributes that we allow filtering on
    some_bool = "some_bool"
    some_str = "some_str"
    some_int = "some_int"


@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: Union[int, bool, str],
) -> List[Widget]:
    widgets = []
    for widget in await Widget.find(
        getattr(Widget, widget_attr) == attr_value
    ).to_list(length=100):
        widgets.append(widget)

    return widgets
```

The data type specified for `attr_value` in the `path` seems to be overriding the function signature (`Union[...]`).  In other words, `attr_value` is always coerced to a string (never int or bool).

Thoughts?
questions.reference_answer:
I tried a couple variations but wasn't able to land on the coercion that I needed to fit my needs.  I think part of it is because of the lack of boolean support for path parameters, but the other part is not being able to coerce between integers/int-like items and strings (I think the docs state this, but maybe I'm just changing my interpretation of the docs after testing).  For example, none of the permutations I tried allowed me to accept a path parameter that would be an integer if I passed "3" and still allow for a string if I passed "foo".

I worked around this by setting the path param data type to string and then letting Pydantic coerce from within the function:

```
class Widget(Document):
    name: str
    some_bool: bool
    some_str: str
    some_int: int


class WidgetByAttr(str, Enum):
    # Set of widget attributes that we allow filtering on
    some_bool = "some_bool"
    some_str = "some_str"
    some_int = "some_int"


class WidgetByAttrValue(BaseModel):
    coerced: int | bool | str

    def __init__(self, coerced: int | bool | str, **kwargs) -> None:
        # Workaround for: https://github.com/starlite-api/starlite/discussions/883
        coerced = coerced.lstrip("/")
        super(WidgetByAttrValue, self).__init__(coerced=coerced, **kwargs)


@get(
    path="/widgets/by-attr/{widget_attr:str}/{attr_value:str}",
    response_description="List Widgets by an attribute value",
)
async def read_widgets_by_attr(
    widget_attr: WidgetByAttr,
    attr_value: str,
) -> List[Widget]:
    coerced = WidgetByAttrValue(attr_value).coerced

    widgets = []
    for widget in await Widget.find(
        getattr(Widget, widget_attr) == coerced
    ).to_list(length=100):
        widgets.append(widget)

    return widgets
```
questions.curate_reason: Concrete technical question with substantive workaround; chunks [0] and [5] directly support the principle that path slot type and handler function type can differ, which is foundational to the answer.

questions.title: Unclear how to create a background task with dependencies from within the handler scope
questions.question:
Unclear how to create a background task with dependencies from within the handler scope

This seems related to #666 but also more specific than that discussion.

Both Starlette and FastAPI have you define background tasks within the view and to pass it as the background parameter to the response object. This seems to be correctly scoped as it allows one to do background things with arbitrary resources that have been variously injected into, created within, or retrieved within the handling scope.

In Starlite, because the background function is [referenced in the route decorator](https://starlite-api.github.io/starlite/usage/5-responses/11-background-tasks/), it is not clear how one would pass in locally scoped resources as arguments to the background task.

The immediate solution that comes to mind is to [return a Starlette JSONResponse](https://starlite-api.github.io/starlite/usage/5-responses/3-returning-responses/#returning-other-library-responses) and use the above mentioned pattern of specifying the background parameter in the response. However, I am currently [having issues with rendering Starlette's JSONResponse](https://github.com/starlite-api/starlite/issues/762) and I think it may break the openapi docs if I do get it working (I'm not entirely sure about this latter point).

I am guessing that [replacing Starlette responses](https://github.com/starlite-api/starlite/pull/626) will be the change that enables the pattern I am looking for here. Should I just wait for that, or is there a currently known approach for what I am trying to do with background tasks?

Thanks!
questions.reference_answer:
This would be a documentation issue. You can add background tasks to your response as well, see the reference docs [here](https://starlite-api.github.io/starlite/reference/response/0-base/#starlite.response.base.Response.__init__). 

So you can do something like

```python
from starlite import Response, get, BackgroundTask


async def do_something_with_id(id: str) -> None:
    ...


@get("/with-background/{id:str}")
async def with_background(id: str) -> Response:
    return Response(
        content="",
        background=BackgroundTask(do_something_with_id, id=id),
    )
```

It's not advertised in the section about background tasks though, which it should be. I guess that's be something that should be covered in a tutorial section on background tasks, what do you think?
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question with a substantive answer showing how to pass locally-scoped resources to background tasks via Response's background parameter and BackgroundTask kwargs.

questions.title: Difference between BackgroundTask, Middlewares and After Request hook
questions.question:
Difference between BackgroundTask, Middlewares and After Request hook

Hello, everyone.

I am a novice web developer and am currently working on a Starlite-based pet project.

I don't really understand the difference between BackgroundTask, Middleware, and After Request hook. It feels like these concepts duplicate behavior, but that's probably because I don't understand them very well. Can someone explain the difference to me?

Thanks a lot in advance!
questions.reference_answer:
> Hello, everyone.
> 
> I am a novice web developer and am currently working on a Starlite-based pet project.
> 
> I don't really understand the difference between BackgroundTask, Middleware, and After Request hook. It feels like these concepts duplicate behavior, but that's probably because I don't understand them very well. Can someone explain the difference to me?
> 
> Thanks a lot in advance!

Hi @IntrepidCrow. 

So there is a degree of overlap between these, but there are also key differences. 

A `BackGroundTask` is simply a sync/async callable that is executed once the response is done sending. It has no access to request and and it cannot be defined on different layers of the application.

An `AfterResponseHookHandler` on the other hand receives the request instance as a parameter and you can "layer" it on the application, so in principle you can declare it even top level on the Starlite constructor for the entire application. You can of course do quite a few similar things in these two, but there are things you can do with an AfterReesponse handler that you cannot do with a background task. Think even of a simple example as logging values from the request instance.

As for Middleware - You can do the same as you can with the other two using middleware, but manipulating responses there is low level and more involved. Furthermore, granular control of which handlers are going to be affected by a middleware is also a bit more involved. 

All in all though, Starlite does not restrict you to use only one pattern - you can choose which one suits your use case best. I would suggest to consider the above in making this determination.
questions.curate_reason: Concrete usage question about Litestar's three execution patterns with a substantive answer; chunks [1], [4], and [6] directly explain background tasks and after_response hooks.
